2026-06-06T23:02:44.0847644Z [36;1mset -euo pipefail[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0848067Z [36;1m# actions/download-artifact (>=v5) flattens contents directly into `path/`[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0848597Z [36;1m# whenever exactly one artifact matches the pattern, ignoring[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0849478Z [36;1m# `merge-multiple: false`. Re-shape into the per-group subdir layout so[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0850055Z [36;1m# downstream validation sees a stable structure regardless of matrix size.[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0850478Z [36;1m# Upstream reference:[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0850929Z [36;1m# https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/blob/v8.0.1/src/download-artifact.ts[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0851556Z [36;1m# (see the `isSingleArtifactDownload || mergeMultiple || artifacts.length === 1`[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0852124Z [36;1m# branch). Remove this step once download-artifact stops flattening or[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0852558Z [36;1m# exposes an opt-out.[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0852937Z [36;1mexpected_count=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq '.group // [] | length')[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0853356Z [36;1mif [ "$expected_count" -eq 1 ]; then[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0853729Z [36;1m  group_id=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq -r '.group[0].id')[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0854213Z [36;1m  # Defence-in-depth: group_id is interpolated into a shell path. apm-prep[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0854738Z [36;1m  # produces a sanitised id today, but enforce a strict allowlist here so[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0855220Z [36;1m  # any future schema drift cannot smuggle traversal sequences.[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0855669Z [36;1m  if ! printf '%s' "$group_id" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$'; then[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0856152Z [36;1m    echo "::error::unsafe group_id '$group_id' (must match ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$)"[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0856534Z [36;1m    exit 1[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0856744Z [36;1m  fi[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0857067Z [36;1m  group_dir="/tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles/${ARTIFACT_PREFIX}apm-${group_id}"[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0857466Z [36;1m  if [ ! -d "$group_dir" ]; then[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0857949Z [36;1m    mkdir -p "$group_dir"[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0858441Z [36;1m    find /tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -path "$group_dir" -exec mv {} "$group_dir/" \;[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:44.0859179Z [36;1m  fi[0m

2026-06-06T23:02:56.7670300Z [36;1m# Generate a secure random API key (360 bits of entropy, 40+ chars)[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:56.7670772Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:56.7671184Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:56.7671530Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-06-06T23:02:56.7671801Z [36;1m[0m

2026-06-06T23:02:56.7672197Z [36;1m[0m

2026-06-06T23:08:00.8674967Z       "rationale": "The bug was silent; the fix is also silent. After this PR, when `apm marketplace add <pkg>` reads an existing apm.yml and finds N transitive deps, it says nothing. A user who hit #1666 and upgrades a targeted outreach to dual-format package maintainers. (2) Dual-format is the path apm plugin init creates on day one -- meaning every user of the official quickstart was exposed; release note should call APM has no way to confirm the fix is working without inspecting the lockfile manually. npm prints each package it resolves; cargo announces 'Downloading N crates'; pip prints the resolution tree. A single verbose-level line like this out explicitly, not bury it. (3) EPAM contributor = enterprise signal; nurture this contributor relationship. Follow-up hypothesis: audit marketplace index for dual-format packages with non-trivial dep graphs to see if any were shipping truncated lockfiles in production."

2026-06-06T23:08:35.1855151Z   └ Completed
2026-06-06T23:08:35.1855404Z 
2026-06-06T23:08:41.8642348Z ly and deps are merged, the output to agents running with --verbose is zero. Resolution decisions should be traceable: `_logger.debug(\"Loaded existing apm.yml for '%s'; preserving dep keys: %s\", plugin_path.name, list(existing_manifest.get('dependencies', {}).keys()))` after line 223 costs nothing at runtime and gives AI agents (and human operators with --verbose) a clear trail. 2026-06-06T23:08:41.8647616Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/deps/plugin_parser.py",
2026-06-06T23:08:41.8655962Z       "line": 222,
2026-06-06T23:08:45.0309218Z       "suggestion": "After `existing_manifest = data`, add: `_logger.debug(\"Merging existing apm.yml for '%s' into synthesized manifest (preserving %d dep blocks)\", plugin_path.name, len(existing_manifest.get('dependencies') or {}))`. This is verbose-only (stdlib logger has no handlers in non-debug runs per the module docstring at line 67)."
2026-06-06T23:08:45.0329142Z     },
2026-06-06T23:08:45.0329659Z     {

Unlike `registries`, which is validated by _parse_registries_block when APMPackage.from_apm_yml loads the result, `scripts` values undergo zero content validation in this code path. A compromised plugin maintainer can embed any shell payload in scripts; if APM ever adds postinstall/prepare hooks, all existing dual-format installs would retroactively become code-execution surfaces without any user consent gate.",

The root-level RegistryPackageResolver is built exclusively from ctx.apm_package.registries (resolve.py:404), so the plugin's registries are currently isolated from resolution. However: (1) the synthesized apm.yml now carries attacker-controlled registry URLs on disk; (2) if any future code path treats the plugin's APMPackage.registries as a resolution authority (e.g., sub-resolver for transitive deps), those URLs activate silently. 2026-06-06T23:12:00.8001339Z     },
2026-06-06T23:12:00.8002028Z     {
2026-06-06T23:12:00.8002924Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-06T23:12:02.0876292Z       "summary": "Existing apm.yml dependencies are now an active dep-injection surface, previously inert due to bug #1666",
2026-06-06T23:12:06.8744617Z       "rationale": "Before this PR, normalization silently discarded all apm.yml deps (the bug). Attackers who placed malicious URLs in apm.yml of a dual-format plugin were inert — APMPackage.from_apm_yml at apm_resolver.py:908 would load an empty or minimal dep list. After this PR, merged_deps (lines 831-858) are faithfully written to the synthesized apm.yml and will be resolved and materialized transitively. This is the correct bug fix behavior, but it elevates apm.yml to the same injection authority as plugin.json without any corresponding visibility: users auditing a Claude plugin typically examine plugin.json, not apm.yml. A compromised plugin maintainer or a malicious PR that only touches apm.yml can now inject arbitrary git URLs into the install graph. No log/warning is emitted when deps are merged from existing_manifest.",

This improves auditability and makes the behavior visible to security-conscious users. Also consider adding a note to enterprise/security.md that dual-format packages expose both plugin.json and apm.yml as dependency injection surfaces."
2026-06-06T23:12:09.2899437Z     },

2026-06-06T23:12:51.4055697Z       "rationale": "Lines 217-225 of synthesize_apm_yml_from_plugin() read the existing apm.yml and set existing_manifest=None on any parse error (except Exception: pass). When existing_manifest is None, the entire dep-merging block at lines 831-836 is skipped. The result is identical to the pre-fix behavior: all dependencies.apm, mcp, lsp are silently discarded. A user whose checked-in apm.yml has a YAML syntax error (e.g. a tab character, a merge-conflict marker) would see the same 'no transitive' symptom as #1666 and have no indication why. The fix is specifically designed to prevent this class of silent loss, but the fallback path re-introduces it. This is a graceful-degradation test, not a dep-preservation test, so the assertion should be that the function does not raise and produces a parseable output — not that deps are preserved (they provably cannot be from malformed input).",

2026-06-06T23:12:57.0178407Z       "summary": "Dict-form existing dependencies (e.g. {apm: {name: '>=1.0'}}) are silently skipped — no test for this shape",
2026-06-06T23:13:01.2855215Z       "rationale": "Lines 833-836 of _generate_apm_yml() gate on `isinstance(val, list)`. If the existing manifest carries dependencies in dict form — e.g. `dependencies: {apm: {package-name: '>=1.0'}}` — the isinstance check fails and the entire existing dep block is silently excluded from merged_deps. The PR trade-offs section documents dict-form handling for PLUGIN deps (plugin.json declares deps as a dict), but does not address dict-form EXISTING manifest deps. A user upgrading a package whose apm.yml uses dict-style deps (a format some tooling generates) would silently lose all existing deps after synthesis — the same symptom as #1666. 2026-06-06T23:13:36.1428004Z       "rationale": "_generate_apm_yml() was a pure transformation: (plugin manifest dict) -> YAML string. Adding existing_manifest couples a second concern -- merging pre-existing state -- into the same function, making the merge logic harder to test in isolation and the function harder to reason about (now 90+ lines). The PR body correctly identifies the merge as a distinct step but implements it inside the generator rather than between load and generate. Design patterns used in this PR: none -- straight-line procedural code, appropriate for the scope. Pragmatic suggestion: extract a standalone _merge_existing_into(plugin_manifest: dict, existing: dict) -> dict pure-dict helper (no I/O, no YAML). Call it from synthesize_apm_yml_from_plugin() between load_yaml() and _generate_apm_yml(). _generate_apm_yml() stays a pure plugin-only generator; the merge becomes a separately-testable step. At this scope a dataclass is not needed -- a plain dict->dict function is the simplest correct design.",

2026-06-06T23:19:41.5673012Z ```json
2026-06-06T23:19:41.5673440Z {
2026-06-06T23:19:43.9447754Z   "headline": "Fix restores the README headline promise of transitive dependency resolution for dual-format packages by making synthesize_apm_yml_from_plugin() non-destructive; every marketplace and remote-subdir install colocating plugin.json with apm.yml now correctly preserves its transitive deps.",
2026-06-06T23:20:05.6553121Z   "arbitration": "Panel converges strongly on two pre-ship items and three follow-up concerns. The fix is correct and materially important: the README central promise -- transitive dependency resolution, just like npm or pip -- was silently broken for every dual-format package until this PR. The code change is targeted and the 8 new tests in TestSynthesizePreservesExistingManifest provide a solid regression trap for the core #1666 scenario, even though the sandbox could not run-certify them (outcome: unknown per test-coverage-expert S7 contract; treated as structured opinion, not load-bearing evidence).\n\nThe single strongest signal across the panel is the bare except Exception: pass at line 224, flagged independently by four active panelists (python-architect, cli-logging-expert, devx-ux-expert, supply-chain-security-expert) and confirmed as an untested fallback gap by test-coverage-expert (outcome: missing on a portability-by-manifest surface, inheriting near-blocking weight per CEO evidence-weighting rules). This line silently re-introduces the exact symptom of #1666 under a YAML corruption trigger: the user loses all transitive deps with zero diagnostic output, indistinguishable from pre-fix behavior. Every other error path in this file that discards data calls _surface_warning() at lines 404, 424, and 568; this path breaks that established contract. The fix is one line: except Exception as exc: _surface_warning(f'Could not load existing apm.yml for merge; transitive deps may be lost: {exc}', _logger). A regression-trap test for the malformed-YAML fallback must accompany it. The missing CHANGELOG.md entry is the second pre-ship item: CONTRIBUTING.md requires it for behavior changes, the [Unreleased] section currently has entries for #1662 and #1664 but nothing for #1666, and two panelists flag this gap independently. A fix that restores the project headline promise but has no CHANGELOG entry breaks release-comms traceability and wastes the community-trust signal this contribution earns.\n\nSupply-chain raises two forward-looking concerns -- scripts blocks preserved verbatim with no lifecycle isolation and registries blocks carrying potentially attacker-controlled URLs on disk -- but neither is exploitable today: APM does not auto-execute scripts and plugin registries are isolated from root resolution. Stripping these fields would introduce new data loss for dual-format authors who rely on them, which is the exact failure mode this PR fixes. The correct path is a tracked follow-up issue designing an explicit allowlist for scripts lifecycle keys and a registry URL authority check against the root project allowlist. The python-architect SRP concern and the dict-form dep silencing gap (flagged by two panelists and missing a regression-trap test per test-coverage-expert) are real but scope-bounded; handle in a targeted follow-up to keep this merge focused on the regression it is fixing.",
2026-06-06T23:20:13.7133983Z   "dissent_notes": "supply-chain-security-expert recommends stripping scripts and registries from the preserved-blocks tuple entirely, citing forward risk from lifecycle-hook auto-execution and attacker-controlled registry URLs persisted on disk. No other active panelist supports stripping. The CEO sides against: both risks are non-exploitable today, stripping would introduce new data loss for dual-format authors, and a threat model with no active exploitation path does not justify regressing the fix's data-fidelity goal. Tracked follow-up with explicit allowlist design is the correct response.\n\ntest-coverage-expert classifies the missing PR body Scenario Evidence table as a recommended finding with outcome: missing (static tier). This is a rubric process finding, not a code or behavior defect. The 8 integration-tier tests are substantive coverage for the core regression; the table is process overhead that does not influence the merge decision. Downweighted to nit.\n\ntest-coverage-expert returned outcome: unknown for the 8 new TestSynthesizePreservesExistingManifest tests with a stated explanation (sandbox S7 contract, no run-certify capability). 2026-06-06T23:28:09.3104616Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3105451Z   None of these appear in the actual changed files. Now I'm running through the fallback self-check to see if this PR modifies any authentication behavior, token handling, credential resolution, or authorization mechanisms.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3106339Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3109112Z   The core change here is making `synthesize_apm_yml_from_plugin()` preserve existing `apm.yml` content for dual-format packages by reading the file first, then merging critical blocks like dependencies, registries, and scripts. The registries block preservation caught my attention—while it does contain registry URLs, the actual authentication still flows through the normal AuthResolver, so this is just preserving configuration data without changing how tokens get resolved or how requests get authorized. I should verify this by checking the actual diff to confirm my assessment.

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3263969Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3264783Z   4. Fast-path files are unchanged, and the PR doesn't alter authentication behavior, token management, credential resolution, host classification, authorization headers, or fallback semantics, so I'm returning `active: false`.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3265593Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3267440Z [174] ◆ Diff confirms: both changed files (`plugin_parser.py`, `tests/unit/test_plugin_parser.py`) contain zero imports from any auth module, no token access, no `os.getenv` for credentials, no `Authorization` header construction, and no `AuthResolver` / `TokenManager` call sites. The `registries` block preservation is purely structural YAML pass-through — the preserved dict is written back to `apm.yml` as-is and would still be consumed by the normal `AuthResolver` at request time, unchanged.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3269452Z

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3275377Z

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3276450Z

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3278687Z

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3279955Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3280128Z

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3281269Z   6. **Duplicate dedup**: The union logic relies on `==` equality for deduplication.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3281656Z

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3310590Z [196] ◐ Now I have a good understanding of the code. Let me look at the full context around the `except Exception: pass` block and compare it with the `_surface_warning` pattern used elsewhere in the file:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3311362Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3311772Z   1. Line 224-225: The new code has `except Exception: pass  # Best-effort; fall back to plugin-only metadata`
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3312438Z   2. Lines 404-408, 424-428, 568: `_surface_warning()` is already called elsewhere for similar cases
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3313084Z   3. Lines 61-75: `_surface_warning()` emits to BOTH the stdlib logger AND the rich console (`_rich_warning`)
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3313541Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3313718Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3315669Z   4. The real problem is that when `apm.yml` exists but fails to load, the user gets no warning at all—the exception is silently swallowed and the code falls back to stripping dependencies without any feedback. Additionally, when the merge path succeeds, there's no debug logging to indicate that dependencies were preserved from the existing manifest, which matters for users running with `--verbose`. The silent `pass` isn't just cosmetic; it masks a failure case that should be visible to the user.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3318797Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3320591Z   5. Looking at the broader pattern in the codebase, lines 404, 424, and 568 all use `_surface_warning()` for similar "skipping due to error" scenarios, so the new exception handler should follow that same convention rather than silently passing.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3321584Z

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3346723Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3346929Z   The main findings I have so far:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3347727Z   1. **CHANGELOG.md does NOT have an entry for #1666** - the grep returned no matches. The Unreleased section has entries for #1662 and #935/#1664, but not #1666.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3349626Z   2. **No docs describe dual-format packages** (plugin.json + apm.yml). The `package-types.md` reference describes the `plugin.json` layout but only says it "maps to runtime directories via `_map_plugin_artifacts`" -- it doesn't mention that packages can have BOTH `plugin.json` AND `apm.yml`, or what happens to transitive deps in that case.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3351015Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3351199Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3351941Z   3. The `installing-from-marketplaces.md` page correctly mentions "transitive resolution" as a capability of `apm install`, and this fix makes that claim accurate again, so no doc updates are needed there.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3353116Z   4. The `manage-dependencies.md` page already covers transitive dependencies comprehensively with tools like `apm deps why`, so it's fine as-is.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3353952Z   5. The PR body itself is well-written with clear WHY/WHAT/HOW sections and solid validation evidence.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3354415Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3356130Z   The main findings are that CHANGELOG.md is missing an entry for #1666, and more importantly, `reference/package-types.md` doesn't explain that packages can declare both `plugin.json` and `apm.yml` together, or that transitive dependencies in `apm.yml` will be resolved in that dual-format setup. This is a real gap for users authoring Claude plugins with dependencies who wouldn't know they need the `apm.yml` file to declare transitive deps. I should also check if there's documentation about remote subdir installs.

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3391102Z [239] ◐ I now have enough context to form my growth-hacker opinion on this PR. Let me synthesize the findings:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3391581Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3393238Z   1. **Ecosystem adoption signal**: Marketplace installs silently dropping transitive deps is a serious trust-killer. The fix is critical. The README explicitly advertises "transitive dependency resolution, just like npm or pip" - this fix is what actually delivers that promise for dual-format packages. Without this fix, anyone who tried to use a marketplace plugin with transitive deps would get a broken experience, silently.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3394769Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3395240Z   2. **Contributor funnel**: This is a community contributor fix (sergio-sisternes-epam from EPAM). It's a solid-scope fix with:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3396005Z      - Clear issue traceability (#1666 → #1687)
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3396373Z      - Well-scoped to 2 files (plugin_parser.py + test file)
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3396833Z      - Excellent test coverage (8 regression tests across different scenarios)
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3397234Z      - Detailed docstring updates
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3397569Z      - This is an excellent model for a community contribution
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3397893Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3399258Z   3. **Release signaling**: The CHANGELOG [Unreleased] section is missing this fix - a significant omission given that the README explicitly promises "transitive dependency resolution, just like npm or pip" and this fix delivers that promise for dual-format packages. It should be a prominent "[Fixed]" entry in the CHANGELOG.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3400474Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3401670Z   4. **Dual-format package authoring**: The fix legitimizes dual-format packages (plugin.json + apm.yml) as a supported, non-destructive pattern. The `first-package.md` documentation mentions `apm plugin init` creates both files together, but doesn't explicitly explain what happens when both exist and how transitive dependencies are preserved.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3402942Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3404123Z   5. **Marketplace ecosystem health**: If this bug existed in production, there could be dual-format marketplace packages with silently dropped transitive dependencies. Package authors testing via `apm install` might have seen truncated dependency graphs, and a follow-up health check or migration note could help identify affected packages.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3405361Z

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3443702Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3444071Z [252] ◐ Now I have enough information to provide my UX review. Let me analyze the PR from a DevX/UX lens.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3444525Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3444711Z   ## Key Findings:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3444911Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3445228Z   1. **Silent fallback on malformed YAML (`except Exception: pass`)** - Line 224:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3446442Z      - This is the most significant UX concern. If a user's `apm.yml` is malformed YAML, the fix silently falls back to plugin-only metadata, still dropping all transitive dependencies. The user would still see the exact same symptom as the bug they thought was fixed, with no indication of *why*.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3447942Z      - From npm/pip/cargo perspective: if your `package.json` or `Cargo.toml` is malformed, the tool screams at you immediately. It never silently drops your dependencies.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3449738Z      - There's already a `_surface_warning()` helper in this file that routes messages through both the standard logger and rich console, but it's not being used here. At minimum, this should surface a warning to tell users their `apm.yml` couldn't be parsed and they might be losing transitive dependencies.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3450851Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3451869Z   2. **No positive signal when transitive deps are found** - The fix silently preserves transitive dependencies after previously discarding them, but doesn't give users any feedback that this is happening. There's no message like "Found 3 transitive deps via..." to confirm the fix is working.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3453225Z      - Other package managers like npm, cargo, and pip all print what they're installing or resolving, so this is a notable gap.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3454099Z      - This is a recommended improvement rather than blocking—the behavior is correct but invisible to the user.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3454592Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3455543Z   3. **Idempotency** - The deduplication logic checks if an entry already exists before appending, which works correctly for string equality. Even with dict-form entries where key ordering might differ, Python's dict equality is order-insensitive, so this handles it fine.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3456567Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3456922Z   4. **Mental model alignment** - The fix aligns with how npm handles transitive dependencies.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3457356Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3457982Z   5. **Recovery guidance** - The silent exception handling is the real issue here. If the YAML is malformed, the user gets no feedback... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3238 chars]
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3458604Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3458720Z [253] ◆ ```json
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3459050Z   {

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3459519Z     "active": true,
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3460215Z     "summary": "Fix restores correct transitive resolution mental model; one silent-failure surface remains where malformed apm.yml still drops deps without any user-visible warning.",

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3464349Z         "rationale": "The bare `except Exception: pass` at line 224-225 means a user whose apm.yml has a YAML syntax error will still hit the exact symptom described in #1666 -- lockfile stops at depth 1 -- with zero diagnostic output. npm, pip, and cargo all treat a corrupt manifest as a hard, loud failure. After this PR, when `apm marketplace add <pkg>` reads an existing apm.yml and finds N transitive deps, it says nothing. A user who hit #1666 and upgrades APM has no way to confirm the fix is working without inspecting the lockfile manually. npm prints each package it resolves; cargo announces 'Downlo... 2026-06-06T23:28:09.3491488Z [263] ◐ This is key. The `ctx.apm_package` refers to the **root** project's `apm.yml`, not the plugin's synthesized `apm.yml`. The resolve phase uses `ctx.apm_package.registries` for registry resolution, which is the *root project's* `apm.yml`. The synthesized plugin `apm.yml` is written to disk but not automatically re-loaded as the resolver context.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3492721Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3493001Z   Let me verify this by checking how `ctx.apm_package` is populated.

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3495470Z [266] ◐ Now I have all the information I need to provide a comprehensive review. Let me summarize my findings:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3495948Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3496247Z   1. **Diagnostic gap - silent `except Exception: pass` (lines 224-225)**: 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3496919Z      - This is inconsistent with the established `_surface_warning()` pattern used elsewhere in the file (lines 404, 424, 568).
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3497729Z      - When `apm.yml` exists but fails to parse, the user silently falls back to the original broken behavior (deps stripped).
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3498454Z      - The `_surface_warning()` helper is already available in this file, and this case should use it.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3499438Z      - This is a **recommended** finding (not blocking) — it's a correctness regression risk when apm.yml is corrupt, but not a security issue.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3500021Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3500199Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3501307Z   2. **Missing verbose logging for successful merges**: When dependencies are successfully preserved from an existing `apm.yml`, there's no debug output to signal this to users running with `--verbose`, which would be useful for understanding whether deps are being merged or freshly synthesized. This is a minor enhancement.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3503117Z   3. **No user-visible distinction between merge and fallback paths**: The user gets no output indicating whether dependencies came from the existing manifest or were generated fresh, making it harder to diagnose behavior differences.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3504000Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3507309Z   Actually, reconsidering the severity here — the silent exception and fallback to stripped deps represents a correctness risk. If the `apm.yml` exists but is malformed, the old bug silently reappears. Though this is an edge case requiring a malformed file, it's still worth flagging as a recommended finding rather than just a nit. I'm settling on "recommended" severity because the happy path works correctly, the malformed YAML case is documented as best-effort, and users would likely encounter other failures from a corrupted manifest anyway. Now I need to finalize my findings: the silent exception handler in `plugin_parser.py` should surface a warning to match the error-handling pattern used elsewhere in the file, since swallowing the exception means users can... 2026-06-06T23:28:09.3676768Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3680308Z [341] ◐ Now I have a complete picture. The `registries` block from the synthesized plugin `apm.yml` is read by `APMPackage.from_apm_yml()` at line 908 in `apm_resolver.py`, but it's NOT used for resolution—only `ctx.apm_package.registries` (the root project's registries map) is used to instantiate `RegistryPackageResolver`. The plugin's `registries` field would be in the loaded `APMPackage` object, but since the `RegistryPackageResolver` is instantiated only once from the root registries map, the plugin's registries don't influence resolution.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3682253Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3682439Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3684046Z   Looking at the `scripts` block, I see that `ScriptRunner` reads scripts from the current directory's `apm.yml` rather than from a plugin's version, and the install pipeline doesn't automatically execute postinstall hooks—`scripts` is a user-facing feature like `npm run` that requires explicit invocation. Now I need to examine the `_union_dep_list` function and how it handles dependencies.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3685445Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3686466Z   The core security concern is whether a plugin's existing `apm.yml` can inject malicious dependencies through the merge process. The `registries` block gets preserved from the plugin's `apm.yml`, which could potentially allow an attacker to redirect package resolution to a malicious registry.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3687559Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3691085Z   However, I need to trace through the actual resolver logic more carefully. When a transitive package is loaded, its `APMPackage` object includes the `registries` from the synthesized `apm.yml`, but the question is whether those registries actually get used during dependency resolution or if only the root project's registries are consulted. So when the plugin's `APMPackage` is created from its synthesized `apm.yml`, it includes whatever `registries` block is defined there, but the resolver only consults the global `ctx.apm_package.registries` when fetching transitive dependencies. This means the plugin's own registry configuration gets stored in the `APMPackage` object but never actually gets used during resolution—unless there's somewhere else in the pipeline... Looking at the dict-form existing deps handling, lines 833-836 check whether values are lists. which won't overwrite existing apm deps. The existing test covers list form, but there's no specific test for dict-form plugin deps combined with existing apm deps where `setdefault` preserves what's already there—severity is low since it's an internal code path. I also notice `_union_dep_list` uses equality comparison which handles dicts correctly in Python, though there's no explicit test for deduplicating dict entries, and the existing test inclu... 2026-06-06T23:28:09.3723316Z [354] ◐ Now I have a clear picture. Let me also check if there's an integration test for the specific scenario of an existing LSP dep.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3723874Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3724141Z   Let me now also confirm the tier classification. Looking at:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3725474Z   1. The new tests in `tests/unit/test_plugin_parser.py::TestSynthesizePreservesExistingManifest` - they call `synthesize_apm_yml_from_plugin` with `tmp_path` (real filesystem, no mocks). This directly exercises the install normalization pipeline with real fixture files. This qualifies as `integration-with-fixtures` tier.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3726697Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3727339Z   2. `test_validate_apm_package_dual_format_preserves_deps` actually calls `validate_apm_package` -- this is in the "full chain" and qualifies as `integration-with-fixtures` clearly.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3728051Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3728226Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3730239Z   The tests are positioned in `tests/unit/` but they're actually operating at the `integration-with-fixtures` tier, which is exactly what's needed for install pipeline coverage—that's a positive finding. However, there's a gap: the silent exception handler at line 224 that strips dependencies when malformed YAML is encountered has no test coverage, which means users could experience silently broken installs without any warning.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3731784Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3733870Z   There's also an issue with how dict-form dependencies are handled—if an existing manifest uses `dependencies: {apm: {name: version}}` instead of a list, that entire branch gets skipped and existing deps vanish. Similarly, while LSP dependencies are technically preserved in the same loop as apm/mcp deps, there's no test confirming that `dependencies.lsp` from an existing manifest actually survives the synthesis process, which is concerning given that the original bug was about exactly this kind of silent stripping.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3735691Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3737270Z   When both the existing manifest and plugin provide apm deps in dict form, `setdefault` gives priority to the existing ones—this behavior is documented but the interaction is surprising and untested. There's also a gap around deduplication: the existing test includes dict entries like `{"git": "parent", "path": "packages/stack-js"}` but doesn't specifi... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2656 chars]
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3738607Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3739174Z [355] ✓ github-pull_request_read(method: get, owner: microsoft, repo: apm, pullNumber: 1687)
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3739552Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3740216Z [356] ✓ $ grep -n "TestSynthesizePreservesExistingManifest\|existing.*lsp\|lsp.*existing\|_lsp_deps.*test\|test.*_lsp" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/tests/unit/test_plugin_parser.py
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3740859Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3741103Z [357] ◐ Now I have a complete picture. Let me synthesize my findings:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3741463Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3742469Z   1. **Dependency injection via `existing_manifest.dependencies`**: The existing `apm.yml`'s `dependencies` block is merged with the plugin-only metadata. Since the existing `apm.yml` is a file in the plugin's repo, it's at the same trust level as `plugin.json`. No new attack surface here, BUT:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3743532Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3744156Z      - Before this PR, the bug silently discarded `apm.yml` deps → no transitive deps would be resolved
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3744795Z      - After this PR, the bug is fixed → the `apm.yml` deps ARE resolved
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3746259Z      - If a plugin had a malicious `apm.yml` with poisoned dependencies that were previously ignored, they'd now get resolved and installed. This is the intended fix, but it does expand the attack surface—the `apm.yml` becomes an active supply chain injection vector instead of being inert during plugin normalization.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3747568Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3748650Z   Severity: **recommended** — the dependencies are in the same repo as `plugin.json`, so they share the same trust level. The key concern is that `apm.yml`'s transitive dependencies weren't previously materialized, so auditing tools and users wouldn't have caught them.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3749880Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3751746Z   The `registries` block from an existing `apm.yml` gets preserved in the synthesized version, but there's an important distinction: the root resolver only uses registries from the ROOT project, while a plugin's registries block gets parsed when loading it as a transitive dependency. The URL validation in `_parse_registries_block()` rejects anything not starting with `https://` or `http://`, blocking `file://` or `data://` injection attempts. However, the validation only checks the prefix, so an attacker could potentially craft a malicious HTTPS URL.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3753642Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3755171Z   The critical finding is that even though a plugin's registries are loaded and preserved in the synthesized output, they don't actually influence dependency resolution—only the root project's registries map is used to instantiate the `RegistryPackageResolver`. The plugin's registries block is essentially inert in the resoluti... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3994 chars]
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3756461Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3757046Z [358] ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/apm/apm && grep -rn '"includes"\|\.includes\b\|includes:' src/apm_cli/ --include="*.py" | grep -v __pycache__ | grep -v test | head -20

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3762573Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3762792Z [364] ◐ Now I have a good picture. Let me analyze the findings:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3763126Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3764415Z   1. **Architecture soundness**: The merge-in-place approach within `_generate_apm_yml()` is reasonable for this bug fix. The fix is minimal and targets the right place. However, adding `existing_manifest` to `_generate_apm_yml()` conflates two concerns: generation from plugin metadata, and merging with existing manifests. Not a blocking concern, just an architectural smell.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3765762Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3767536Z   2. **`_generate_apm_yml()` signature change**: Adding `existing_manifest` to this function is a pragmatic choice. The alternative would be a separate `_merge_manifests()` function called from `synthesize_apm_yml_from_plugin()`. The current approach has `_generate_apm_yml()` doing both generation AND merging, which violates single responsibility, but the scope is small enough that it's not a blocking concern. A pragmatic suggestion would be a separate `_merge_manifests()` helper.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3769501Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3769675Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3770623Z   3. **`_union_dep_list()` placement**: This utility function lives at the module level in `plugin_parser.py`, which is fine for a pure helper with no plugin state dependencies. It could theoretically move to `utils/` or become a static method, but that's not necessary at this scope.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3772784Z   4. **`except Exception: pass`**: There's a real issue here—if `load_yaml()` fails, we silently fall back to plugin-only metadata without logging anything. We should use the existing `_surface_warning()` helper to at least surface what went wrong, though the current approach isn't blocking.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3774566Z   5. **"target" vs "targets" ambiguity**: Both fields can coexist in the preservation list, and the loop correctly preserves whichever is present. If a manifest has both (which the model allows), they both get preserved, faithfully round-tripping the original structure.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3775546Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3776717Z   6. **Dict-form dependency handling**: The code silently skips non-list dependency values, but there's an inconsistency—plugin.json can declare dependencies as a dict mapping names to versions, yet the preservation logic drops these non-... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 9842 chars]
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3776730Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3777025Z [365] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/de..., view_range: [60, 80])
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3777029Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3777845Z [366] ✓ $ grep -n "ConflictingTargets\|target.*conflict\|targets.*conflict" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/models/apm_package.py /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/core/target_detection.py 2>/dev/null | head -20
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3777850Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3778034Z [367] ◐ Now I have a full picture. Let me summarize:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3778121Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3779528Z   1. `includes` is an "include-only manifest" field that controls which repo paths to publish (`auto` or a list of paths). It does NOT read external files at install time — it's a publish-time governance flag, not a filesystem include directive. So preserving `includes` from the existing `apm.yml` is safe.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3779630Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3780593Z   2. `scripts` is stored as `dict[str, str] | None` in APMPackage. The scripts field maps script names to shell commands. The `ScriptRunner` executes these when the user runs `apm run <script>`. During install, there's no auto-execution of scripts. So preserving `scripts` is safe from an auto-execution standpoint.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3780673Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3780746Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3782070Z   However, by keeping `scripts` from the existing `apm.yml` in the synthesized version, I'm embedding potentially arbitrary shell commands without validation. While they won't auto-execute now, this creates a forward-looking risk if APM adds lifecycle hooks in the future. Now I'm compiling my final security findings, starting with the `registries` block being merged verbatim without URL validation at merge time.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3782149Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3785133Z   The `registries` block gets validated later when `APMPackage.from_apm_yml()` is called, which enforces `https://` or `http://` prefixes and rejects unknown fields. But the actual URL content—like `https://attacker.com`—isn't checked against any allowlist, and the plugin's registries don't actually govern resolution anyway since the root project's registries take precedence. The risk is that the synthesized `apm.yml` now carries untrusted registry URLs, and if future changes start using transitive packages' registries for resolution, these could accidentally get activated. I should either strip the `registries` from the synthesized output entirely or add a comment explaining that the plugin's registries are intentionally unused for resolution.
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3785226Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3785880Z   Now looking at the second finding: before this PR, `apm.yml` dependencies were silently discarded during plugin normalization, so malicious deps were harmless. Af... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4694 chars]
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3785885Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3786234Z [368] ◆ Now I have sufficient context. Let me compile the findings.

2026-06-06T23:28:09.3805651Z [386] ✓ $ python3 -c " import json finding_1 = { 'severity': 'recommended', 'summary': 'Dict-form existing deps silently dropped; inconsistent with plugin-derived dict deps which ARE preserved', 'rationale': ( 'Lines 834-836: the loop \`for key, val in existing_deps.items(): if isinstance(val, list): merged_d...
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3805664Z 
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3805903Z [387] ◐ Now I have everything I need. Let me formulate the JSON findings:
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3805979Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3806254Z   1. **`registries` block from plugin apm.yml preserved without URL allowlist check** (recommended)
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3806419Z      - File: `src/apm_cli/deps/plugin_parser.py`, line 863-873
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3806845Z      - The registries block from the existing `apm.yml` is blindly copied into the synthesized manifest
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3807336Z      - URL validation happens downstream at `APMPackage.from_apm_yml()` (must start with https:// or http://) but that's format validation, NOT authority validation
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3807916Z      - The registry URLs from a plugin are NOT used by the root-level RegistryPackageResolver (which reads only from ctx.apm_package.registries), so this is not an active vulnerability today
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3809153Z      - However, the synthesized apm.yml now carries attacker-controlled registry URLs that could be exploited if the pipeline ever treats this file as a configuration authority, so I should recommend either stripping the registries block from the synthesized output or adding a defensive comment documenting the isolation
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3809237Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3809493Z   2. **`dependencies` from existing apm.yml now active dep injection surface** (recommended)
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3809655Z      - File: `src/apm_cli/deps/plugin_parser.py`, lines 831-845
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3810247Z      - This PR changed the behavior: previously plugin normalization discarded apm.yml deps (bug #1666) and the existing apm.yml was inert during install, but now those dependencies become active
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3810888Z      - The existing apm.yml's dependencies are merged and resolved transitively through apm_resolver.py, meaning an attacker controlling the plugin's apm.yml can inject arbitrary package URLs that get installed
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3811531Z      - While the trust level matches plugin.json since they're in the same repo, apm.yml is less visible to users auditing a Claude plugin, and there's no validation beyond what APMPackage.from_apm_yml() applies later
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3811866Z      - I should recommend adding a log or warning when deps from an existing apm.yml are merged to improve auditability
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3811949Z   
2026-06-06T23:28:09.3812153Z   3. **`scripts` block preserved without lifecycle isolation** (recommended)

APMPackage._parse_dependency_dict() also skips non-list values (`if not isinstance(dep_list, list): continue`), so there is no runtime crash -- but the written apm.yml silently diverges from the original, risking data loss if any future tooling can consume dict-form dep values. Adds a comment to an existing GitHub issue, pull request, or discussion. Use this to provide feedback, answer questions, or add information to an existing conversation. For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 2 comment(s) can be added. Supports reply_to_id for discussion threading.","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"body":{"description":"The comment text in Markdown format. Must be the final intended comment — not a placeholder or test value. This is the 'body' field - do not use 'comment_body' or other variations. Provide helpful, relevant information that adds value to the conversation.","type":"string"},"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"item_number":{"description":"The issue, pull request, or discussion number to comment on. If omitted, the tool will attempt to resolve the target from the current workflow context (triggering issue, PR, or discussion). If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.","type":["number","string"]},"pr":{"description":"Alias for item_number when targeting a pull request. If the given node ID belongs to a nested reply, the handler automatically resolves it to the top-level parent. Only applicable for discussion comments — ignored for issue and pull request comments.","type":"string"},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"},"temporary_id":{"description":"Unique temporary identifier for this comment. The temporary ID is returned in the tool response so you can reference this comment later.","pattern":"^aw_[A-Za-z0-9]{3,12}$","type":"string"}},"required":["body"],"type":"object"},"name":"add_comment"},{"description":"Remove labels from an existing GitHub issue or pull request. If a label is not present on the item, it will be silently skipped. Use this to clean up labels that are no longer applicable. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 3 label(s) can be removed. Only these labels can be removed: [panel-review panel-approved panel-rejected].","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"item_number":{"description":"Issue or PR number to remove labels from. If omitted, removes labels from the item that triggered this workflow.","type":"number"},"labels":{"description":"Label names to remove (e.g., ['bug', 'needs-triage']). Labels that don't exist on the item are silently skipped. At least one label must be provided.","items":{"type":"string"},"minItems":1,"type":"array"},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"}},"required":["labels"],"type":"object"},"name":"remove_labels"},{"description":"Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted. When a bash command is blocked by security policy, call this tool with reason set to \"security\".","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"alternatives":{"description":"Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).","type":"string"},"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"reason":{"description":"Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).","type":"string"},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"},"tool":{"description":"Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"message":{"description":"Status or completion message to log. to specify what pull request data needs to be retrieved from GitHub. \nPossible options: \n 1. get - Get details of a specific pull request.\n 2. get_diff - Get the diff of a pull request.\n 3. get_status - Get combined commit status of a head commit in a pull request.\n 4. get_files - Get the list of files changed in a pull request. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.\n 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. Returns threads with metadata (isResolved, isOutdated, isCollapsed) and their associated comments. Use cursor-based pagination (perPage, after) to control results.\n 6. get_reviews - Get the reviews on a pull request. When asked for review comments, use get_review_comments method.\n 7. get_comments - Get comments on a pull request. Use this if user doesn't specifically want review comments. Check runs are the individual CI/CD jobs and checks that run on the PR.\n","enum":["get","get_diff","get_status","get_files","get_review_comments","get_reviews","get_comments","get_check_runs"],"type":"string"},"owner":{"description":"Repository owner","type":"string"},"page":{"description":"Page number for pagination (min 1)","minimum":1,"type":"number"},"perPage":{"description":"Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)","maximum":100,"minimum":1,"type":"number"},"pullNumber":{"description":"Pull request number","type":"number"},"repo":{"description":"Repository name","type":"string"}},"required":["method","owner","repo","pullNumber"],"type":"object"},"name":"pull_request_read"},{"annotations":{"readOnlyHint":true,"title":"Search code"},"description":"Fast and precise code search across ALL GitHub repositories using GitHub's native search engine. 2026-06-06T23:28:09.6236596Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-06T23:03:38.514Z","event":"rpc_request","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"OUT","server_id":"github","method":"tools/call","payload":{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/call","params":{"arguments":{"method":"get","owner":"microsoft","pullNumber":"1687","repo":"apm"},"name":"pull_request_read"}}}
2026-06-06T23:28:09.6252689Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-06T23:03:39.119Z","event":"rpc_response","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"IN","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":1687,\"title\":\"fix(deps): preserve transitive dependencies in marketplace and remote subdir installs (closes #1666)\",\"body\":\"## TL;DR\\n\\nPlugin normalisation was silently overwriting `apm.yml` with identity-only fields, stripping all dependencies from dual-format packages. This broke transitive resolution for marketplace and remote subdirectory installs.\\n\\n## Problem (WHY)\\n\\nWhen installing a package that has both `plugin.json` (or `.claude-plugin/`) and `apm.yml`, the package type detection cascade classifies it as `MARKETPLACE_PLUGIN` and triggers `synthesize_apm_yml_from_plugin()`. This function **unconditionally overwrites** the existing `apm.yml` with a synthesized version containing only identity fields (`name`, `version`, `description`, `author`, `license`, `type`), discarding:\\n\\n- `dependencies.apm` and `dependencies.mcp` -- breaking transitive resolution\\n- `devDependencies` -- breaking dev install flows\\n- `registries`, `targets`, `includes`, `scripts` -- losing resolution-critical metadata\\n\\nThis manifests as two user-facing symptoms reported in #1666:\\n1. **Marketplace install** says \\u0026#34;no transitive\\u0026#34; and the lockfile only contains the direct dep\\n2. **Remote subdir install** stops at depth 1 for `git: parent` monorepo sibling chains\\n\\nBoth are the same root cause: the resolver reads the stripped manifest, finds no sub-dependencies, and stops.\\n\\n## Approach (WHAT)\\n\\nMake plugin normalisation **non-destructive** for dual-format packages:\\n\\n1. Before overwriting, load the existing `apm.yml` via `load_yaml()`\\n2. Generate plugin-derived fields (identity, MCP/LSP deps from `mcpServers`)\\n3. **Merge** existing resolution-critical blocks into the synthesized manifest\\n4. Union dependency lists without duplicates\\n\\nPlugin identity fields override (name, version, etc.). Dependency lists are unioned. Resolution-critical blocks (`devDependencies`, `registries`, `targets`, `includes`, `scripts`) are preserved from the original manifest.\\n\\n## Implementation (HOW)\\n\\n### `src/apm_cli/deps/plugin_parser.py`\\n\\n- **`synthesize_apm_yml_from_plugin()`** -- reads existing `apm.yml` before overwriting and passes it to `_generate_apm_yml()` as `existing_manifest`\\n- **`_generate_apm_yml()`** -- new `existing_manifest` parameter; starts from existing deps as base, then layers plugin-derived deps via `_union_dep_list()`\\n- **`_union_dep_list()`** -- new helper that appends entries to a dep list without duplicates, handling both string and dict entries (e.g. `{git: parent, path: ...}`)\\n\\n### `tests/unit/test_plugin_parser.py`\\n\\n8 new regression tests in `TestSynthesizePreservesExistingManifest`:\\n1. Preserves existing `dependencies.apm` and `dependencies.mcp`\\n2. Synthesised manifest is loadable by `APMPackage.from_apm_yml()` with non-empty deps\\n3. Merges plugin MCP deps with existing apm.yml deps (union, no duplicates)\\n4. No-existing-apm.yml case unchanged (no regression)\\n5. Preserves `devDependencies`\\n6. Preserves `registries`, `targets`, `scripts`, `includes`\\n7. `.claude-plugin/` directory without `plugin.json` preserves deps\\n8. Full `validate_apm_package()` chain on dual-format package preserves deps\\n\\n## Trade-offs\\n\\n- **Merge semantics**: existing `apm.yml` entries take priority in the union. Plugin-derived deps that duplicate existing entries are skipped. This is the safe default -- the apm.yml is the author\\u0026#39;s intent.\\n- **Dict-format plugin deps**: plugin.json `dependencies` as a dict (`{\\u0026#34;name\\u0026#34;: \\u0026#34;^version\\u0026#34;}`) is preserved as-is under `dependencies.apm` (matching pre-existing behaviour), while list-format deps go through the union path.\\n\\n## Validation evidence\\n\\n| Check | Result |\\n|-------|--------|\\n| `ruff check src/ tests/` | Clean |\\n| `ruff format --check src/ tests/` | Clean |\\n| `pylint R0801` (duplication) | 10.00/10 |\\n| `lint-auth-signals.sh` | Clean |\\n| New tests (8) | All pass |\\n| Full unit suite | 16,638 passed, 2 skipped |\\n\\n## How to test\\n\\n```bash\\n# Run the new regression tests\\nuv run --extra dev pytest tests/unit/test_plugin_parser.py::TestSynthesizePreservesExistingManifest -v\\n\\n# Run the full plugin parser suite (84 tests)\\nuv run --extra dev pytest tests/unit/test_plugin_parser.py -v\\n\\n# Reproduce with the reporter\\u0026#39;s repo (requires network)\\napm marketplace add cisco-ge
2026-06-06T23:28:09.6267701Z ...
